title: BIN Validation Pre-Flight Pattern with Card Type Verification Probing
narrative: Fraudster systematically queries card type and issuer metadata before each transaction attempt by submitting $0.00 or $1.00 authorization-only requests with varying BINs. Pattern shows 12-40 micro-transactions within 90 seconds, each testing different first-six digits, followed by 2-8 genuine purchase attempts at $89-$340 within 3-5 minutes using only BINs that validated successfully. All probes originate from identical device fingerprint (DeviceInfo hash) and IP address (dist1=0 across sequence). Failed validations show declined_auth codes, while successful probes immediately precede purchase attempts with matching card1 prefixes. The card1_txn_count_1day shows 18-45 events clustered in single session, with card1_amt_zscore spiking to 8.2+ on actual purchase attempts after probe phase completes.
indicators: $0.00-$1.00 authorization requests in rapid succession (12-40 within 90 seconds)
card1 first-six varies across sequence while DeviceInfo and dist1 remain constant
Declined authorization codes on 70-85% of micro-transactions
Purchase attempts ($89-$340) within 180-300 seconds using only validated BINs
Identical P_emaildomain and dist1=0 across entire probe-to-purchase sequence
card1_amt_zscore jumps from <0.5 during probes to 8.2+ on purchase attempts
summary: Dozens of $0-$1 authorization requests test different card BINs in under 90 seconds from the same device and IP, followed by real purchase attempts only using the BINs that validated successfully.
reasoning: The fraudster is mapping which stolen card numbers are active before committing to actual purchases, minimizing detection by only attempting transactions with cards they've confirmed will process.
indicator_explanations: Attacker fires dozens of tiny test charges to find working cards
Different card numbers tested from same device and location simultaneously
Most probe transactions fail as attacker discovers which cards are dead
Real purchase attempts follow immediately after finding cards that validate successfully
Single card attempts many transactions in tight time clusters during validation
Same email and location used throughout entire validation and purchase sequence
Spending jumps dramatically from tiny probe amounts to large fraudulent purchases
